questions.q: The 2016 DNC hack began with a spear phishing email sent to which individual?
options: John Podesta, Hillary Clinton's campaign chairman
Hillary Clinton, the presidential candidate

questions.q: True or False: The display name in an email is a reliable way to verify who actually sent it.
options: True — email providers verify display names
False — the real sender is shown in the actual email domain, not the display name

questions.q: What is spear phishing?
options: A mass phishing campaign sent to millions of random recipients
A targeted phishing attack using personal details to appear credible to a specific victim

questions.q: Which email authentication failure is the strongest indicator that a message was not sent by the claimed domain?
options: DKIM, SPF, and DMARC all failing
The subject line containing urgent language

questions.q: True or False: Business Email Compromise (BEC) is the most financially damaging cybercrime category, costing $2.7 billion per year.
options: True
False — ransomware causes more financial damage than BEC

questions.q: True or False: Accounts protected by two-factor authentication are 99.9% less likely to be compromised than accounts without it.
options: True
False — the protection benefit is closer to 50%

questions.q: Why is SMS-based 2FA considered weaker than an authenticator app?
options: SMS codes expire faster than TOTP codes, making them harder to use
SMS codes can be intercepted via SIM swapping, while authenticator apps are immune to this attack

questions.q: How did the attacker compromise Uber's systems in the 2022 breach?
options: By exploiting a software vulnerability in Uber's VPN server
By spamming push notification approvals until a contractor clicked Approve out of frustration

questions.q: True or False: TOTP codes generated by authenticator apps work by communicating with the server in real time to receive each code.
options: True — codes are sent from the server to the app
False — both the server and app independently compute the same code using a shared secret and the current time

questions.q: What attack is number-matching 2FA specifically designed to defeat?
options: MFA fatigue attacks, where attackers spam push notifications hoping for an accidental approval
SIM swapping attacks that redirect SMS codes to the attacker's phone

questions.q: True or False: A long passphrase made of four random words is generally stronger than an 8-character password with complex substitutions like P@ssw0rd.
options: True — length increases entropy exponentially and substitutions are in attackers' dictionaries
False — complex character substitutions make shorter passwords more resistant to cracking

questions.q: What was the most significant outcome of the 2009 RockYou breach?
options: It proved that bcrypt hashing makes passwords uncrackable at scale
32 million passwords stored in plaintext were exposed, creating rockyou.txt — the most used password cracking wordlist

questions.q: At 100 billion guesses per second, approximately how long would it take to crack a password with 71 bits of entropy?
options: A few seconds to a few minutes
Approximately 750 years

questions.q: True or False: Salting a password hash prevents rainbow table attacks by ensuring two users with the same password have different stored hashes.
options: True
False — salting only slows down brute-force attacks, not rainbow table lookups

questions.q: Which hashing algorithm is most appropriate for storing user passwords in a modern application?
options: MD5 or SHA-1, because they are fast and widely supported
bcrypt or Argon2id, because they are deliberately slow and resistant to GPU-based cracking

questions.q: True or False: On an open WiFi network, an attacker must send packets to your device to capture your traffic.
options: True — active injection is required to intercept traffic
False — passive listening captures all unencrypted frames without sending a single packet

questions.q: What protection does HTTPS provide on a public WiFi network?
options: It encrypts the content of web traffic, though the destination domain remains visible via SNI
It fully encrypts all traffic including DNS queries and hides the destination domain entirely

questions.q: How did the Darkhotel APT group compromise executives on hotel WiFi networks?
options: By stealing hotel WiFi passwords using brute-force attacks against the router
By sitting on the hotel network as a man-in-the-middle and injecting malicious fake software update prompts into HTTP connections

questions.q: True or False: On WPA2 personal WiFi, if you know the network password you can decrypt other users' traffic.
options: True — all devices on a WPA2 personal network share the same encryption key
False — WPA2 uses per-session keys that prevent other users from decrypting your traffic

questions.q: Which option provides the strongest protection when you must use an untrusted network?
options: Using HTTPS-only websites, which encrypts content and hides all metadata
Using your phone's mobile hotspot, which creates an isolated 4G/5G channel away from shared WiFi

questions.q: True or False: Social engineering attacks require exploiting a software vulnerability to succeed.
options: True — attackers need a technical exploit entry point
False — social engineering manipulates people directly, bypassing all technical controls

questions.q: In the Facebook/Google BEC scam, how did attacker Evaldas Rimasauskas steal over $120 million?
options: By hacking into the companies' financial systems using malware
By sending fake invoices impersonating a real supplier that both companies regularly paid

questions.q: Which psychological lever does urgency exploit in social engineering attacks?
options: It overrides the victim's rational decision-making by creating panic and time pressure
It makes the victim feel they are being watched and must comply to avoid punishment

questions.q: True or False: Legitimate IT support staff will sometimes need your password to help diagnose and fix account issues.
options: True — IT teams require credentials to access accounts for troubleshooting
False — legitimate IT never needs your password; a request for it is a red flag

questions.q: What is the correct way to verify the identity of someone claiming to be from your IT department or a security agency?
options: Ask them for their employee ID number and write it down for your records
Hang up and call back using a known official number, not the one they provided

questions.q: True or False: Most real-world cyberattacks exploit zero-day vulnerabilities that have no available patch.
options: True — attackers primarily rely on unknown vulnerabilities
False — most attacks exploit known, patchable vulnerabilities because most systems are poorly patched

questions.q: The WannaCry ransomware infected 200,000 computers in 2017. What was the key factor that made this possible?
options: The attackers discovered a novel zero-day that no one knew about
A patch for the exploited vulnerability (MS17-010) had been available for two months but was not applied

questions.q: What does a CVSS score of 9.8 indicate about a vulnerability?
options: It is a critical severity vulnerability, likely allowing remote code execution with no authentication
It is a moderate severity vulnerability that requires local access to exploit

questions.q: True or False: Log4Shell (CVE-2021-44228) required attackers to have prior access to a system before they could exploit it.
options: True — Log4Shell required authenticated access to trigger
False — sending a single specially crafted string that got logged was enough to trigger remote code execution

questions.q: What is a zero-day vulnerability?
options: A vulnerability unknown to the vendor for which no patch yet exists
A vulnerability that was patched on the same day it was publicly disclosed

questions.q: True or False: A data breach only puts you at risk if you made a security mistake with that company.
options: True — breaches only expose users who had weak passwords
False — your data is exposed whenever the company holding it is breached, regardless of your own security practices

questions.q: What is credential stuffing?
options: An attack where stolen username and password pairs from one breach are tested automatically against other sites
A technique for guessing passwords by trying every possible combination of characters

questions.q: How long did Yahoo's 2013 breach of 3 billion accounts go undetected before disclosure?
options: Approximately three years, discovered in 2016
Approximately six months, discovered in early 2014

questions.q: True or False: The Have I Been Pwned API uses k-anonymity, meaning your actual password is never transmitted when checking if it has been exposed.
options: True — only the first 5 characters of the SHA-1 hash are sent to the API
False — the full password hash is sent to the server for comparison

questions.q: Why are security question answers particularly dangerous when exposed in a data breach?
options: Security questions are harder to change than passwords, and the answers often remain valid across accounts indefinitely
Security question answers are stored in plaintext, making them easier to crack than hashed passwords

questions.q: Given the URL https://monterey-aquarium.org.phish-site.ru/tickets — what is the actual domain?
options: monterey-aquarium.org, since that appears first in the URL
phish-site.ru, because the real domain is always the rightmost portion before the first slash

questions.q: True or False: An HTTPS padlock in the browser means a website is safe and not a phishing site.
options: True — HTTPS proves the site is legitimate
False — HTTPS only proves the connection is encrypted; phishing sites routinely use HTTPS with valid certificates

questions.q: In Operation Aurora, how did attackers compromise Google employees' devices?
options: By sending email attachments that required the victim to manually download and run a file
By directing targets to a malicious website that exploited an Internet Explorer zero-day via a drive-by download requiring no additional clicks

questions.q: True or False: URL shorteners like bit.ly always reveal their final destination in the link preview before you click.
options: True — all major URL shorteners display the destination URL on hover
False — URL shorteners hide the real destination; you should expand them using tools like curl or unshorten.me before clicking

questions.q: Which method is most reliable for safely checking whether a suspicious URL is malicious?
options: Opening the URL in a private or incognito browser window to limit tracking
Scanning the URL with VirusTotal or Google Safe Browsing before visiting it

questions.q: True or False: Most apps genuinely need always-on location access to function correctly.
options: True — always-on access is required for apps to deliver timely location-based features
False — almost no apps need always-on location; 'While Using' is sufficient for the vast majority

questions.q: How did Cambridge Analytica harvest data from 87 million Facebook users without most of them consenting?
options: They hacked Facebook's database directly using a SQL injection vulnerability
A personality quiz app exploited Facebook's API to collect not just the quiz-taker's data but also all of their friends' data

questions.q: What is the principle of data minimization in the context of privacy?
options: Compressing personal data files so they take up less storage space on servers
Sharing only the minimum amount of personal information necessary, because every piece shared becomes a potential breach exposure

questions.q: True or False: Data brokers compile profiles including addresses, phone numbers, and relatives' names that can be used for targeted spear phishing.
options: True
False — data brokers only sell anonymized aggregate statistics, not individual profiles

questions.q: Why is sharing your real phone number for SMS 2FA a potential privacy and security risk?
options: Phone numbers can be used for SIM swapping attacks, where attackers transfer your number to steal SMS codes
Phone numbers allow apps to track your location without needing location permission

questions.q: According to the NIST SP 800-61 incident response framework, what should be your first action after discovering a compromise?
options: Containment — stop the attacker's active access before investigating the full scope
Root cause analysis — understand how the attacker got in before taking any other action

questions.q: True or False: Changing your password after an account compromise is sufficient to fully evict an attacker.
options: True — a password change immediately terminates all attacker access
False — attackers can maintain access through OAuth app tokens and email forwarding rules that survive password resets

questions.q: What was the entry point for the Colonial Pipeline ransomware attack?
options: A zero-day vulnerability in the pipeline's industrial control system software
A single compromised VPN password with no 2FA enabled on the account

questions.q: True or False: During incident response, documenting the timeline and evidence should wait until after full recovery is complete.
options: True — documentation is a post-recovery activity that should not distract from remediation
False — documentation should happen throughout the response; it supports root cause analysis, legal action, and improving defenses

questions.q: Which of these is an Indicator of Compromise (IOC) that should trigger immediate investigation?
options: Receiving a software update notification from a known vendor through the app's built-in update mechanism
Receiving a password reset email you did not initiate, or seeing unfamiliar devices listed in your account sessions
